coredata.dc:title: Development of a pH-Responsive, SO4 2–-loaded Fe and N co-doped carbon quantum dots-based fluorescent method for highly sensitive detection of glyphosate
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Zoujun
Zeng, Min
Wu, Sihao
Yan, Ziyu
Rui, Jiahan
Qiu, Ping
Wang, Xiaolei
coredata.dc:description: A novel sulfate-loaded iron-nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (SO4 2–-CQDs)-based fluorescent method was synthesized by the facile and environmentally friendly pyrolysis of persimmon frost (carbon source) and (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O. After SMMC-7721 cells were incubated with the SO4 2–-CQDs for 24 h, more than 95% of the cells remained viable, even at a high concentration of the SO4 2–-CQDs, indicating excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity. In addition, it was able to be taken up by the cells to emit their bright blue fluorescence after excitation at 365 nm, indicating suitable cell permeability. The SO4 2–-CQDs also exhibited a unique response to changes in pH, which was applied in the detection of OPs by relying on the production of acetic acid from the hydrolysis of acetylcholine (ACh) by acetylcholinesterase (AChE), which decreased the pH and engendered an increase in the fluorescence of the SO4 2–-CQDs; however, the inhibition of AChE by glyphosate resulted in little influence on fluorescence intensity due to the lack of acetic acid produced. This mechanism was the basis for the development of a sensitive assay for the detection of glyphosate. The resulting assay had a limit of detection of 0.066 ng/mL. Furthermore, the method was successfully applied for the precise and accurate monitoring of the concentration, distribution, and variation of glyphosate residues in chives and cultivated soil. Therefore, the proposed method was anticipated to provide a promising alternative for other detection methods to enable the reliable analysis of OPs in food products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organophosphorus pesticide
Glyphosate
Carbon quantum dots
Acetylcholinesterase
Cell toxicity
Degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of glyphosate based on the SO4 2–-CQDs
Detection of glyphosate in chives and cultivated soil
Working principle of the SO4 2–-CQDs for OPs method
Detection of glyphosate in chives and soil

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21765015 , 21808099 ), China and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province ( 20192BCD40001 ), China. At the same time, thanks to Mr. Pucheng Zou and Mrs. Heying Deng for the guidance of the actual sample planting. Also, the authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the XPS analysis.

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21765015, 21808099), China and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province (20192BCD40001), China. At the same time, thanks to Mr. Pucheng Zou and Mrs. Heying Deng for the guidance of the actual sample planting. Also, the authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) High-resolution TEM image of SO4 2–-CQDs. (B) TEM image and size distribution of SO4 2–-CQDs. (C) FTIR spectra of SO4 2–-CQDs and CQDs. (D) XPS spectra of the SO4 2–-CQDs and high-resolution C1s (E), N1s (F), Fe2p (G), S2p (H).

(A) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of SO4 2–-CQDs, with the inset (1) and (2) being the natural light image of SO4 2–-CQDs and fluorescent image under 365 nm light illumination, respectively. (B) Fluorescence spectra of SO4 2–-CQDs under the excitation of different wavelengths. (C) Fluorescence intensity of SO4 2–-CQDs in 6 weeks. (D) Excitation intensity of SO4 2–-CQDs in buffer changing from pH 3.0 to pH 7.0 for 5 times.
The SMMC-7721 cells' images of bright field, fluorescence and merge with SO4 2–-CQDs at 25 and 100 μg/mL in 40, 80 and 120 min, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of SO4 2–-CQDs versus different pH values. (B) Fluorescence spectra of SO4 2–-CQDs versus different pH values. (C) The sensing mechanism of the SO4 2–-CQDs-based method for glyphosate.

The factors of the concentration of AChE (A) and ACh (B), time (C) affecting value (F–F
0
)/F
0 . (D) Sensitivity of determination of glyphosate. (E) Relationship between the logarithm of glyphosate concentration and the fluorescence intensity of the method. (F) The selectivity of the detection system.
Images of chives (A) splashed with glyphosate and (B) contrast. (C) Images of the leaves, stems, roots of chives. (D) Images of the cultivated soil.
Comparison of our method and other fluorescence methods for OPs analysis.
Detection results on the expression level of glyphosate in leaves, stems and roots of chives and cultivated soil splashed with glyphosate.

head.ce:title: Development of a pH-Responsive, SO4 2–-loaded Fe and N co-doped carbon quantum dots-based fluorescent method for highly sensitive detection of glyphosate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Study, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Advanced study, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel sulfate-loaded iron-nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (SO4 2–-CQDs)-based fluorescent method was synthesized by the facile and environmentally friendly pyrolysis of persimmon frost (carbon source) and (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O. After SMMC-7721 cells were incubated with the SO4 2–-CQDs for 24 h, more than 95% of the cells remained viable, even at a high concentration of the SO4 2–-CQDs, indicating excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity. In addition, it was able to be taken up by the cells to emit their bright blue fluorescence after excitation at 365 nm, indicating suitable cell permeability. The SO4 2–-CQDs also exhibited a unique response to changes in pH, which was applied in the detection of OPs by relying on the production of acetic acid from the hydrolysis of acetylcholine (ACh) by acetylcholinesterase (AChE), which decreased the pH and engendered an increase in the fluorescence of the SO4 2–-CQDs; however, the inhibition of AChE by glyphosate resulted in little influence on fluorescence intensity due to the lack of acetic acid produced. This mechanism was the basis for the development of a sensitive assay for the detection of glyphosate. The resulting assay had a limit of detection of 0.066 ng/mL. Furthermore, the method was successfully applied for the precise and accurate monitoring of the concentration, distribution, and variation of glyphosate residues in chives and cultivated soil. Therefore, the proposed method was anticipated to provide a promising alternative for other detection methods to enable the reliable analysis of OPs in food products.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) have been among the most widely used pesticides in the agricultural industry to prevent or control pests or other dentate animals from damaging plants and crops throughout every stage from planting to harvesting and transportation [1–3]. OPs are popular among pesticides because they are inexpensive, widely available, and highly efficiency [4]. However, their widespread usage has contributed to a significant of the pesticides in the ecosystem, resulting in toxicity that has created global environmental and health concerns [3]. In humans and other animals, the toxicity is manifested by their ability to inhibit acetylcholinesterase (AChE), which is an essential enzyme in the nervous system that metabolizes the neurotransmitter acetylcholine (ACh). The accumulation of ACh can lead to organ failure and even death [5–7]. To limit the risk of human consumption and ensure food safety, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has unauthorized the use of OPs [3], and the European Commission (EC) has set a general minimal risk level (MRL) of 0.01 mg/kg for OPs in food products [8]. Therefore, the quantitative determination of the distribution and variation of OP residues in plants, including crops (e.g. vegetables), and soil is essential for ensuring proper food safety and environmental protection.

Currently, the most common techniques for the detection of OPs include liquid chromatography (LC) [9], gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy (GC-MS) [10], and liquid chromatography-mass spectroscopy (LC-MS) [11]. Although these techniques are capable of selectively detecting OPs, they involve complex processes, are time-consuming, and require skilled technicians. In contrast, fluorescence spectroscopy is relatively trivial and convenient and provides a rapid response; therefore, fluorescence is regarded as a reliable detection method for the detection of OPs [12–15]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbonaceous fluorescent nanomaterials that have become increasing popular in scientific research because they are small in size but have a large surface area, are highly photostable, have low cytotoxicity, are environmentally friendly, and have unique photoluminescence properties [16]. To date, CQDs have been widely applied in sensing, bioimaging, and gene and drug delivery applications [17–22], but they show tremendous promise for myriad other applications because of their low cost and ease of preparation. Various raw materials, such as fruits, vegetables, fishes and food residues, have been used in the preparation of CQDs [23–27], and many other fluorescence sensors for the detection of OPs have been proposed using CQDs by targeting AChE inhibition, fluorescence quenching, or the quencher's unique response to ATCh hydrolysates catalyzed by AChE [13,14,28,29]. Despite the rapid progress on the development of sensors for OP applications, the currently available sensors are governed by some issues, such as using toxic quenchers, synthesizing difficultly and relying on a single mode of detection. Recently, the other hydrolysate CH3COOH could change pH of the monitoring system, which is a new research direction as the response site for OPs detection [30].

In this work, sulfate-loaded iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (SO4 2–-CQDs) were prepared and investigated for their ability to serve as a reliable fluorescent method for precisely monitoring changes in the concentration, distribution, and metabolism of OPs in agricultural crops. The SO4 2–-CQDs were synthesized through pyrolysis of persimmon frost on the surface of persimmon cakes as the carbon source in the presence of (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O. Interestingly, the SO4 2–-CQDs displayed a unique response to acidic pH, which was the basis for targeting acetylcholine (ACh) metabolism by acetylcholinesterase (AChE), as thiocholine and CH3COOH are generated from the hydrolysis of ACh, thereby decreasing the pH and turning on the fluorescence response of the method. Therefore, the presence of glyphosate influenced the activity of AChE and, thus, the production of CH3COOH, which influenced the pH. The SO4 2–-CQDs relied on this mechanism for the reliable detection of OPs. The detection performance of the SO4 2–-CQDs was then assessed for measuring the concentration, distribution, and metabolism of glyphosate in the leaves, stems and roots of chives as well as the cultivated soil.

Glyphosate (98.7%) was purchased from Beijing Pushrm Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and acetylcholine (ACh) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Tris-HCl buffer (20 mM, pH 7.5) was used to dissolve the AChE (987 unit/mL). The solution of ACh (80 mM) was freshly prepared in ultrapure water, and used within 4 h. Ammonium ferrous sulfate hexahydrate ((NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The persimmon frost was scraped from commercially available persimmon cakes. The buffer solutions used in this study were as follows: disodium hydrogen phosphate-citrate buffer (10 mM, pH 7); phosphate buffered saline (PBS) buffer (10 mM). All chemicals from commercial sources were of analytical grade, and the ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm−1, Millipore, USA) was used throughout the experiments.

UV–vis Absorption spectra were recorded using UV-2450 Shimadzu Vis-spectrometer (Japan) and the fluorescence spectra were performed on a F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan), separately. The morphology images and the particle size of SO4 2–-CQDs were obtained using a TF 20 transmission electron microscope (TEM; FEI Tecnai, USA). The functional groups on the surface of SO4 2–-CQDs were collected using a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer from Tianjin Gangdong Sci.&Tech. Co., Ltd. (FTIR-650, Tianjin, China) with KBr pellets at room temperature. The elemental composition of SO4 2–-CQDs was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha electron spectrometer (Thermo, USA). The cell viability was determined by recording the absorbance of formazan at 490 nm on Enzyme-labeled Instrument (Rayto Life Sciences Co., LTD). Cell imaging was analyzed using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Ti-E, Nikon Corporation, Japan) equipped with a 100 W mercury lamp (C-SHG1, Nikon).

The SO4 2–-CQDs were prepared by the pyrolysis of persimmon frost and (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O at 125 °C. Briefly, 1.5 g of persimmon frost and 0.15 g of (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O were heated together at 125 °C for 10 min in a 100 mL round bottom flask until becoming a liquid. After that, the mixture kept stirring at 125 °C for another 5 min, during which the color of the liquid changed from colorless to pale yellow to finally brown. Then, 50.0 mL of a 10 mM NaOH solution in water was added into the mixture, and the resulting mixture was centrifugated at 8,000 rpm for 10 min. The suspension was filtered, and the resulting solid was lyophilized for 48 h. The obtained granular solid was dissolved in ultrapure water to prepare a stock solution (0.1 mg/mL) for characterization and other applications. The CQDs were synthesized using the same procedure but without adding (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O.

The cellular toxicity of the materials was evaluated using the standard 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay in SMMC-7721 cells (human hepatocarcinoma cell). First, a 96-well plate was seeded with 200 μL of a cell suspension to achieve a cell density of 1 × 104 cells/well, and the cells were cultured at 37 °C for 24 h in an incubator with an atmosphere of 5% CO2. Thereafter, the culture medium was removed, and the cells were treated with DMEM including different concentrations of the SO4 2–-CQDs for 24 h. Subsequently, 100 μL of an MTT solution (5:1) was added to each well, and the plate was incubated for 4 h. After replacing the medium with DMSO (100 μL), the plate was shaken for 10 min, and the absorbance of the solution in each well was recorded through an enzyme-labeling instrument.

The SMMC-7721 cells were seeded in DMEM containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) supplemented with 100 units/mL penicillin and 100 mg/mL streptomycin, the cells were incubated at 37 °C in an 5% CO2 atmosphere. For fluorescence imaging, the cells were incubated with 25 and 100 μg/mL of the SO4 2–-CQDs at 37 °C for 40, 80, and 120 min. Prior to imaging, all the cells were washed three times with PBS (pH = 7.4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glyphosate based on the SO4 2–-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: All AChE reactions were carried out at 37 °C. A total of 40 μL of a 0.8 U/mL AChE solution was reacted with 10 μL of solutions of different concentrations of glyphosate for 30 min. Then, 50 μL of a 100 mmol/L solution of ACh was added and reacted for another 50 min. Finally, 200 μL of the SO4 2–-CQDs solution and 700 μL of disodium hydrogen phosphate-citrate buffer were added before the fluorescence measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glyphosate in chives and cultivated soil
ce:section.ce:para: Two groups of chives were cultivated. One of the groups was sprayed with 25 mL of a 0.20 mg/mL glyphosate solution once every 2 days for 15 days starting on the 9th day. On the 16th day, for the final splash, the glyphosate in the leaves, stems, and roots of the chives, as well as the soil, was extracted. First, 10.00 g of leaves/stems/roots/soil was ground, and the material shook for 20 min after mixing with 100 mL of ultrapure water and 50 mL of methanol. After separating methanol, the mixture was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the supernatant was collected to determine the levels of glyphosate in the various parts of the chive plant and the cultivated soil according to the aforementioned method.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to characterize the preparation of the SO4 2–-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 A and B, the morphology of the SO4 2–-CQDs were spherical, and they exhibited an excellent dispersity in aqueous solution. The inset of Fig. 1B shows that the statistical average size of the spherical particles was 4.78 ± 1.17 nm in diameter. FTIR spectroscopy was employed to determine the identity of the surface functional groups of the SO4 2–-CQDs and the CQDs. In the FTIR spectra of both the SO4 2–-CQDs and CQDs, the absorption band at 3394 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of the O–H group (Fig. 1C). Moreover, the absorbance band at 2358 cm−1 corresponded to the S–H, while the band at 2925 cm−1 indicated the presence of alkyl chain groups (e.g., –CH2– and –CH3) in the SO4 2–-CQDs [31]. Absorption bands at 1652 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 appeared in both of the FTIR spectra and were attributed to the CC stretching vibrations and CO vibrations. The band at 1020 cm−1 corresponded to the characteristic band of the C–O stretching vibrations. Compared to the peaks in the spectrum of the CQDs, the bands at 1456 cm−1 and 1144 cm−1 in the spectrum of the SO4 2–-CQDs were characteristic bands that proved the existence of C–N and SO groups in the modified CQD sample [32]. Moreover, there were multiple peaks in the fingerprint region (500–1000 cm−1), including peaks at 668 cm−1 (C–S bonds) and 609 cm−1 (Fe–O bonds) [33,34], which indicated that Fe, N, and S elements were successfully doped into the CQDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the elemental composition of the CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1D, the full XPS spectrum of the SO4 2–-CQDs featured six peaks centered at 284, 532, 401, 168, 714, and 1072 eV, suggesting the presence and proportion of C (50.87%), O (43.33%), N (3.03%), S (1.03%), Fe (1.38%) and Na (0.35%) in the SO4 2–-CQDs. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1E) was deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 285.0, 286.4, and 287.8 eV, which corresponded to C–C (sp2), C–N/C–O/C–S, and CO bonds, respectively. The high-resolution N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1F) could be deconvoluted into two peaks centered at 399.8 and 401.7 eV, which corresponded to pyridinic N and graphitic N atoms, respectively. Moreover, the high-resolution Fe 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 1G) was deconvoluted into four peaks; the peak centered at 710.5 eV was attributed to Fe 2p3/2, the peak at 724.0 eV was assigned to Fe 2p1/2, and the peaks at 713.0 and 728.0 eV belonged to their satellites, which corroborated the presence of Fe–O bonds in the SO4 2–-CQDs. The presence of Fe–O bonds might be the result of Fe2+ ions being coordinated by the oxygen-containing functional groups in the CQDs [34]. The high resolution S 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 1H) only contained one peak centered at 168.2 eV, which was assigned to the sulfate S atom. These results, combined with the FTIR results, confirmed that Fe and N atoms were successfully doped into the SO4 2–-CQDs and that S was loaded onto the surface of the SO4 2–-CQDs in the form of sulfate ions. The presence of sulfate on the surface the CQDs might improve the pH sensitivity of the CQDs because the additional negative charges perturbed the electronic properties of the SO4 2–-CQDs and altered the affinity of the sulfate ions to protons.

The UV–vis spectrum of the CQDs featured a broad absorption band at 260 nm (Fig. S1), while two typical absorption bands at 226 nm and 284 nm (Fig. 2 A, black curve) were observed in the absorbance spectrum of the SO4 2–-CQDs, which originated from the π-π* transition of aromatic CC and the n-π* transition of the CO/CN bone [35–37], respectively. The presence of these bands suggested a polyheterocyclic structure and corroborated the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the SO4 2–-CQDs. In the excitation and emission spectra of the SO4 2–-CQDs, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were 290 and 358 nm, respectively (Fig. 2A), which were significantly blue-shifted compared to the corresponding maxima (380 nm and 475 nm, respectively) of the CQDs (Fig. S1). Furthermore, when excited at 365 nm, the SO4 2–-CQDs emitted strong blue fluorescence in aqueous solution under UV illumination (Fig. 2A, inset). Moreover, no significant shift of the emission wavelength was observed in the excitation wavelength range of 260–310 nm for the SO4 2–-CQDs (Fig. 2B), illustrating an excitation-independent emission of the SO4 2–-CQDs, which was completely different from the CQDs (Fig. S2) and most other CQDs [38–40]. This excitation-independent fluorescence behavior implied that either the size or the surface state of the formed sp2-hybidized clusters in the SO4 2–-CQDs were more uniform than the unmodified CQDs due to the synergy effect of the doped Fe and N atoms [41,42].

Apart from the bright blue fluorescence, the SO4 2–-CQDs also exhibited high photostability, which is highly advantageous for applications involving detection by illumination. Even after storing for 6 weeks in air, the SO4 2–-CQDs maintained a transparent appearance and strong fluorescence (Fig. 2C). The optical stability of the SO4 2–-CQDs was also investigated by monitoring the change in fluorescence intensity under constant UV irradiation and in solutions with different ionic strengths (Fig. S3). The fluorescence intensity negligibly changed when irradiated under UV light (365 nm) for 0.5 h or when exposed to solutions with high ionic strengths, even up to 350 mg/mL of NaCl, which substantiated the excellent photostability of the SO4 2–-CQDs. The reversibility of the probe is another important factor for practical applications. As shown in Fig. 2D, the fluorescence intensity of SO4 2–-CQDs did not change significantly in 5 times repeatedly by monitoring the dynamic changes in fluorescence intensity at pH 3.0 and pH 7.0. These results demonstrated that the SO4 2–-CQDs are reliable for monitoring changes in the fluorescence intensity in biological samples.

The excellent fluorescence behavior and photostability suggested that the SO4 2–-CQDs would be ideal materials for bioimaging. However, to ensure that the SO4 2–-CQDs do not induce cellular toxicity, the SO4 2–-CQDs were evaluated for their cytoxicity in SMMC-7721 cells as the model cell line using an MTT assay (Fig. S4). After incubating the cells with the SO4 2–-CQDs, more than 95% of the cells survived, even when the cells were exposed to 400 μg/mL of the CQDs for 24 h. These results indicated that the SO4 2–-CQDs exhibited no marked cytotoxicity to the cells at all concentrations tested, highlighting the biocompatibility of the SO4 2–-CQDs. Encouraged by these outcomes, we assessed the in vitro imaging performance of the SO4 2–-CQDs in live cells. As shown in Fig. 3 , the SO4 2–-CQDs were evenly dispersed throughout the cytoplasm, as indicated by the uniformly distributed blue fluorescence. As the concentration of the SO4 2–-CQDs and the incubation time increased, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs significantly increased, reaching a maximum when the cells were incubated with 100 μg/mL SO4 2–-CQDs for 120 min. These results showed that not only could the method be used to label SMMC-7721 cells successfully, but also the fluorescence intensity of SO4 2–-CQDs was dependent on the concentration of the CQDs and the incubation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working principle of the SO4 2–-CQDs for OPs method
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the pH sensitivity of the SO4 2–-CQDs, the change in the fluorescence intensity and profile was compared at different pH's. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity changed slowly over the pH range of 3.0–4.0. However, when the pH increased from 4.0 to 7.0, the intensity decreased sharply, demonstrating a typical acid-mediated fluorescence enhancement behavior. As the pH increased from 7.0 to 9.0, the fluorescence remained stable, albeit at a weak fluorescence intensity. In the pH range of 4.3–6.7, there was a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and pH (R2 = 0.990) (Fig. S5). In addition to the pH-dependent decrease in the fluorescence intensity, the emission maximum of the SO4 2–-CQDs in this pH range demonstrated a blue-shift as the pH increased (Fig. 4B). According to previous studies [43,44], the shift of the emission maximum suggested a change in the structure of the SO4 2–-CQDs, which was derived from the formation of a complex between protons and the functional groups, such as sulfates, thiols, and carboxylates, in the SO4 2–-CQDs. The absorbance maximum also changed as the pH increased from 3.0 to 7.0 in the UV–vis spectra of the SO4 2–-CQDs, which corroborated the formation of this complex Fig. S6.

The sensing mechanism of the SO4 2–-CQDs-based method for glyphosate is depicted in Fig. 4C. When AChE, ACh, and SO4 2–-CQDs are present simultaneously, AChE hydrolyzed ACh into CH3COOH, which decreased the pH, causing an increase in the fluorescence intensity of the SO4 2–-CQDs. However, glyphosate inhibited the activity of AChE, mitigating the production of CH3COOH from the catalytic hydrolysis of ACh, which did not influence the pH of the solution. As a result, the fluorescence intensity was significantly lower compared to in the absence of glyphosate. The linear change in the fluorescence intensity enabled the sensitive and accurate determination of the concentration of the OPs in experimental samples. To demonstrate this, solutions of the SO4 2–-CQDs were supplemented separately with different reagents, including ACh, AChE, ACh + AChE, ACh + AChE + glyphosate, CH3COOH, and glyphosate, and the fluorescence response to these additions was observed. As expected, only a negligible change in the fluorescence intensity was observed when the solutions containing the SO4 2–-CQDs were supplemented with ACh, AChE, and glyphosate, while the fluorescence intensity sharply increased upon the addition of ACh + AChE and CH3COOH. However, when glyphosate was present, the emission intensity decreased compared to the addition of ACh + AChE, confirming our speculation (Fig. S7).

To improve the detection performance of the SO4 2–-CQDs-based method towards OPs, several experimental parameters were optimized. For this, the relative fluorescence intensity, which was represented by (F–F
0
)/F
0, was used to evaluate the sensitivity of the method; F
0 was the initial fluorescence intensity of the SO4 2–-CQDs, and F was the fluorescence intensity after reaction. Considering the important role that AChE plays in the analysis of OPs, we quantitatively investigated the effect of the AChE activity on the sensing performance of the SO4 2–-CQDs at different concentrations of the enzyme. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the concentration of AChE that manifested the maximum sensitivity of the CQDs was 1.0 U/mL. Because the fluorescence intensity of the SO4 2–-CQDs directly correlated to the concentration of CH3COOH produced from the hydrolysis of ACh, which was dependent upon the concentration of ACh and the reaction time, these two parameters were further optimized. As illustrated in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence intensity of the SO4 2–-CQDs gradually increased as the concentration of ACh increased from 20 to 80 mM; however, when the concentration of ACh exceeded 80 mM, the emission intensity of the CQDs decreased slightly before plateauing, implying that the fluorescence change of the SO4 2–-CQDs is only concentration-dependent up to a certain concentration of ACh. Therefore, 80 mM was selected as the optimum ACh concentration for AChE sensing. Using similar optimization procedures, the optimal reaction time was found to be 45 min to enable the maximum change in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 5C).

To ensure the best optimal performance of the method for the detection of OPs, the factors affecting the sensitivity were assessed using a L9(34) orthogonal experimental design Table S1. Based on the aforementioned optimization experiments, the effects of the three parameters (AChE concentration, ACh concentration, and incubation time) on the fluorescence sensitivity of the SO4 2–-CQDs were further explored by analyzing the change in fluorescence when the concentration of AChE was 0.8, 1.0, and 1.2 U/mL; the concentration of ACh was 60, 80, and 100 mM; and the incubation time was 40, 45, and 50 min. F
0
’ represented the fluorescence intensity under the previously ascertained optimal conditions (AChE concentration: 1.0 U/mL; ACh concentration: 80 mM; reaction time: 45 min), and F was the fluorescence intensity after the reaction under the new parameters. The results showed that the optimal performance of the SO4 2–-CQDs-based method was achieved when the concentrations of AChE and ACh were 0.8 U/mL and 100 mM, respectively, and when the reaction time was 50 min. Therefore, these conditions were utilized in subsequent experiments.

Using the newly identified optimal conditions, the SO4 2–-CQDs-based was assessed for its ability to detect OPs in solution. As shown in Fig. 5D, as the concentration of glyphosate increased from 0.1 ng/mL to 10 μg/mL, the maximum absorbance of the SO4 2–-CQDs gradually decreased. From these data, a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the logarithm of the glyphosate concentration (0.1 ng/mL to 10 μg/mL) was achieved Fig. 5E. The resulting linear regression equation was y = −34.64 lg C + 957.28 (R2 = 0.993), from which the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.066 ng/mL based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which was much lower than the maximum limit of glyphosate residues in food samples according to the Chinese national food safety standard (GB/T 2763-2016), the EPA [3], and the EC [8]. The sensing performance of the SO4 2–-CQDs toward glyphosate and other OPs was compared to other reported methods, the results of which are shown in Table 1 . The LOD of the SO4 2–-CQDs-based method was lower than most previously developed methods that rely on carbon dots, organic and polymeric dyes, metal clusters, and metal complexes as signal emitters and AuNPs, Au NCs, and MnO2 nanosheets as fluorescence quenchers; in addition, these methods require tedious procedures and are sensitive to interference from reducing agents in situ. The high sensitivity of the SO4 2–-CQDs-based methods might be attributed to the bright emission and excellent response to at acidic pH's, which is highly advantageous for applications in which only a small concentration of glyphosate is present. Taken together, this SO4 2–-CQDs-based fluorescent methods provided a promising alternative over other technologies for the sensitive detection of OPs in food safety applications.

To evaluate the selectivity of the SO4 2–-CQDs-based methods toward OPs, the fluorescence sensitivity of the assays was assessed in the presence of various interfering substances, such as water, vegetables, and soil, in the detection system with a glyphosate concentration of 1 ng/mL. When the test solution contained Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl−, SO4 2–, CO3 2–, NO3 −, HPO4 2–, H2PO4 −, Vitamin B2 (VB2), glycine (Gly), glucose (Glu), urea, and microbial organic fertilizer (MOF) at 100 times (0.59 μM), no interference from these substances was observed Fig. 5F. Therefore, these common substances did not interfere with the assay with high selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glyphosate in chives and soil
ce:section.ce:para: It was reported that chives had the highest amount of OP residues among vegetables [48]. Therefore, chives were used as a model to determine the concentration of glyphosate in the leaves, stems, and roots of the chives, as well as the cultivated soil, using the SO4 2–-CQDs-based methods. One experimental group of the chives was sprayed with glyphosate, and the other group was not sprayed and, therefore, used as a contrast. Their images were manifested in panels A and B of Fig. 6 . There was no glyphosate residue detected in the extracts (leaves, stems, roots, or soil) of the chives that were not sprayed with glyphosate by the SO4 2–-CQDs-based methods (Fig. S8) and HPLC-MS. In addition, these four extracts demonstrated little influence on the fluorescence emission of the SO4 2–-CQDs. Therefore, a standard addition experiment was performed to characterize the detection precision and accuracy by adding 10, 100, and 500 ng/mL glyphosate to the extracts of the chive leaves. As depicted in Table S2, the resulting recoveries of glyphosate from the spiked extracts were 107.2%, 89.3%, and 101.9%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were 2.31%, 3.18%, and 4.67%, respectively. These high recoveries and low RSD demonstrated that the established methods was reliable in the detection of OPs in practical environmental samples.

In summary, we designed and synthesized a blue-emissive SO4 2–-CQDs-based methods through a simple method. Owing to its distinguished fluorescent properties and outstanding biocompatibility, the as-prepared SO4 2–-CQDs have great promise in cell imaging. Moreover, by combining the ACh, AChE and bright fluorescence emission of SO4 2–-CQDs, we developed a novel pH-responsive fluorescent methods for the detection and monitoring of OPs. The assay was successfully applied in measuring the concentration, distribution, and metabolism of glyphosate residues in chives. Although the SO4 2–-CQDs provides a promising design for the pH-responsive fluorescent methods, they are currently only available for OPs testing. The future research is expected to analyze other contaminants to facilitate real-time investigations.

Zoujun Peng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Min Zeng: Investigation. Sihao Wu: Formal analysis. Ziyu Yan: Validation. Jiahan Rui: Visualization. Ping Qiu: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation. Xiaolei Wang: Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21765015, 21808099), China and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province (20192BCD40001), China. At the same time, thanks to Mr. Pucheng Zou and Mrs. Heying Deng for the guidance of the actual sample planting. Also, the authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2022.340110.
